title: Minnesota Sues Federal Government Over Withheld Evidence in Three Shootings
excerpt: Minnesota state officials and Hennepin County sued the Trump administration, accusing it of actively blocking their investigations into the killings of Renée Good and Alex Pretti and the non-fatal shooting of Julio Sosa-Celis — including refusing to share agent names, training records, and body-camera footage.
content:
Months after three separate federal-agent shootings in Minneapolis during Operation Metro Surge, state investigators had received almost no evidence from federal authorities. In late March 2026, Minnesota and Hennepin County filed suit against the Trump administration.

  • State officials said federal agents initially agreed to cooperate at the scenes of the Good and Sosa-Celis shootings, then federal authorities later took control of all evidence and denied state investigators access.
  • Hennepin County Attorney Mary Moriarty said her office had not been given the names of officers involved in the January 24 Pretti killing, nor access to training records. "When was he trained? What was he trained to do? What's in his personnel file?" she asked publicly.
  • In all three cases, DHS rapidly characterized victims as aggressors. In the Sosa-Celis case, the Justice Department charged the victim with assault — then dropped the charges — and DHS acknowledged two officers had "made untruthful statements" and placed them on administrative leave.
  • A federal judge ordered DHS to produce evidence related to the Good killing within three weeks, though the materials were not to be made public.
"They appear to be actively preventing the state from investigating," said University of St. Thomas law professor Rachel Moran, who studies police accountability.
mediaPlaceholder: Court filing in Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension v. Noem showing withheld evidence categories
